[Welwyn Hatfield](/wiki/Welwyn_Hatfield "Welwyn Hatfield") 7. [Broxbourne](/wiki/Borough_of_Broxbourne "Borough of Broxbourne") 8. [Three Rivers](/wiki/Three_Rivers_District "Three Rivers District") 9. [Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford") 10. [Hertsmere](/wiki/Hertsmere "Hertsmere")

([/ˈhɑːrtfərdʃɪər -ʃər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:UK-Herts.ogg "File:UK-Herts.ogg"), [*HART-fərd-sheer -⁠shər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); often abbreviated **Herts**) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England"). It borders [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire") to the north-west, [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") to the north-east, [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") to the east, [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") to the south and [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire") to the west. The largest settlement is [Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford").

**Hertfordshire** ([/ˈhɑːrtfərdʃɪər -ʃər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:UK-Herts.ogg "File:UK-Herts.ogg"), [*HART-fərd-sheer -⁠shər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); often abbreviated **Herts**) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England"). It borders [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire") to the north-west, [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") to the north-east, [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") to the east, [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") to the south and [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire") to the west. The largest settlement is [Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford").

The county has an area of 634 square miles (1,640 km2) and had an estimated population of 1,236,191 in 2024. Watford is in the south-west of the county, and its other settlements include [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") in the north, the city of [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans") in the centre, and [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead") in the west. For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes Hertfordshire is a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") with ten districts. [Potters Bar](/wiki/Potters_Bar "Potters Bar") in the south-east of the county was [historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") part of [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex").

Hertfordshire centres on the headwaters and upper valleys of the rivers [Lea](/wiki/River_Lea "River Lea") and the [Colne](/wiki/River_Colne,_Hertfordshire "River Colne, Hertfordshire"); both flow south and each is accompanied by a canal. Elevations are higher in the north and west, reaching more than 800 feet (240 m) in the [Chilterns](/wiki/Chilterns "Chilterns") near [Tring](/wiki/Tring "Tring"). Hertfordshire's undeveloped land is mainly agricultural and much of the county is covered by the [Metropolitan green belt](/wiki/Metropolitan_Green_Belt "Metropolitan Green Belt").

Since 1903, [Letchworth](/wiki/Letchworth "Letchworth"), in the far north, has served as the prototype [garden city](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement") while [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") became the first town to expand under [post-war Britain](/wiki/Post-war_Britain "Post-war Britain")'s [New Towns Act 1946](/wiki/New_Towns_Act_1946 "New Towns Act 1946"). [Services](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") have become the largest sector of the county's economy.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Hertfordshire](/wiki/History_of_Hertfordshire "History of Hertfordshire")

The county's landmarks span many centuries, ranging from the [Six Hills](/wiki/Six_Hills "Six Hills") in [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") built by local inhabitants during the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") period, to [Leavesden Film Studios](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Studios_Leavesden "Warner Bros. Studios Leavesden").[[5]](#cite_note-Bettley_Hertford_talk-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The volume of intact medieval and [Tudor buildings](/wiki/Tudor_architecture "Tudor architecture") surpasses London, in places in well-preserved [conservation areas](/wiki/Conservation_area_(United_Kingdom) "Conservation area (United Kingdom)"), especially in [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans"), which includes remains of the Roman town of [Verulamium](/wiki/Verulamium "Verulamium").

In 913, Hertfordshire was the area assigned to a fortress constructed at [Hertford](/wiki/Hertford "Hertford") under the rule of [Edward the Elder](/wiki/Edward_the_Elder "Edward the Elder"). Hertford is derived from the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") *heort ford,* meaning [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer") crossing (of a watercourse). The name Hertfordshire is first recorded in the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* in 1011. Deer feature in many county emblems. Many of the names of the current settlements date back to the Anglo-Saxon period, with many featuring standard placename suffixes attributed to the Anglo-Saxons: "ford", "ton", "den", "bourn", "ley", "stead", "ing", "lett", "wood", and "worth", are represented in this county by Hertford, Royston, Harpenden, Redbourn, Cuffley, Wheathampstead, Tring, Radlett, Borehamwood and Rickmansworth.

There is evidence of human life in Hertfordshire from the [Mesolithic period](/wiki/Mesolithic_period "Mesolithic period"). It was first farmed during the [Neolithic period](/wiki/Neolithic_period "Neolithic period") and permanent habitation appeared at the beginning of the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"). This was followed by tribes settling in the area during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").

Following the [Roman conquest of Britain in AD 43](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), the [Catuvellauni tribe](/wiki/Catuvellauni "Catuvellauni") accepted peace and adapted to the Roman life; resulting in the development of several new towns, including [Verulamium](/wiki/Verulamium "Verulamium") (St Albans) where in c. 293 the first recorded British martyrdom is traditionally believed to have taken place. [Saint Alban](/wiki/Saint_Alban "Saint Alban"), a Romano-British soldier, took the place of a Christian priest and was beheaded on Holywell Hill. His martyr's cross of a yellow saltire on a blue field is reflected in the [flag and coat of arms of Hertfordshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Hertfordshire "Flag of Hertfordshire") as the yellow field to the stag or Hart representing the county. He is the Patron Saint of Hertfordshire.

With the [departure of the Roman Legions in the early 5th century](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain"), the now-unprotected territory was invaded and colonised by the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons"). By the 6th century, the majority of the modern county was part of the [East Saxon](/wiki/East_Saxon "East Saxon") kingdom. This relatively short-lived kingdom collapsed in the 9th century, ceding the territory of Hertfordshire to the control of the West Anglians of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"). The region finally became an English shire in the 10th century, on the merger of the West Saxon and Mercian kingdoms.

In the midst of the Norse invasions, Hertfordshire was on the front lines of much of the fighting. King [Edward the Elder](/wiki/Edward_the_Elder "Edward the Elder"), in his reconquest of Norse-held lands in what was to become [England](/wiki/England "England"), established a "[burh](/wiki/Burh "Burh")" or fort in Hertford, which was to curb Norse activities in the area. His father, [King Alfred the Great](/wiki/King_Alfred_the_Great "King Alfred the Great"), established the River Lea as a boundary between his kingdom and that of the Norse lord [Guthrum](/wiki/Guthrum "Guthrum"), with the north and eastern parts of the county being within the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw"). There is little evidence however of [Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") placenames within this region, and many of the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") features remained intact to this day. The county however suffered from renewed Norse raids in the late 10th to early 11th centuries, as armies led by [Danish kings](/wiki/Danish_king "Danish king") [Swein Forkbeard](/wiki/Swein_Forkbeard "Swein Forkbeard") and [Cnut the Great](/wiki/Cnut_the_Great "Cnut the Great") harried the country as part of their attempts to undermine and overthrow English king [Æthelred the Unready](/wiki/%C3%86thelred_the_Unready "Æthelred the Unready").

### Norman invasion onwards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Norman invasion onwards")]

A century later, [William of Normandy](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") received the surrender of some senior English Lords and Clergy at [Berkhamsted](/wiki/Berkhamsted "Berkhamsted"), before entering London unopposed and being crowned at [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster"). Hertfordshire was used for some of the new Norman castles at [Bishop's Stortford](/wiki/Bishop%27s_Stortford "Bishop's Stortford"), and at [King's Langley](/wiki/Kings_Langley "Kings Langley"), a staging post between London and the royal residence of [Berkhamsted](/wiki/Berkhamsted "Berkhamsted").

The [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") recorded the county as having nine [hundreds](/wiki/Hundred_(country_subdivision) "Hundred (country subdivision)"). [Tring](/wiki/Tring_(hundred) "Tring (hundred)") and [Danais](/wiki/Danais_(hundred) "Danais (hundred)") became one—[Dacorum](/wiki/Dacorum "Dacorum")—from Danis Corum or Danish rule harking back to a [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") not [Saxon](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") past. The other seven were [Braughing](/wiki/Braughing "Braughing"), [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage"), [Cashio](/wiki/Liberty_of_St_Albans "Liberty of St Albans"), [Buntingford](/wiki/Buntingford "Buntingford"), [Hertford](/wiki/Hertford "Hertford"), [Hitchin](/wiki/Hitchin "Hitchin") and [Odsey](/wiki/Odsey "Odsey").

In the later Plantagenet period, St. Albans Abbey was an initial drafting place of what was to become [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"). And in the later Wars of the Roses, St. Albans was the scene of two major battles between the Lancastrians and the Yorkists.

In Tudor times, [Hatfield House](/wiki/Hatfield_House "Hatfield House") was often frequented by Queen Elizabeth I. Stuart King James I used the locale for hunting and facilitated the construction of a waterway, the [New River](/wiki/New_River_(London) "New River (London)"), supplying [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water") to London.

As London grew, Hertfordshire became conveniently close to the English capital; much of the area was owned by the [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") and [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy "Aristocracy"), this [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") helped to boost the local economy. However, the greatest boost to Hertfordshire came during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), after which the population rose dramatically. In 1903, [Letchworth](/wiki/Letchworth "Letchworth") became the world's first [garden city](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement") and [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") became the first town to redevelop under the [New Towns Act 1946](/wiki/New_Towns_Act_1946 "New Towns Act 1946") ([9 & 10 Geo. 6](/wiki/9_%26_10_Geo._6 "9 & 10 Geo. 6"). c. 68).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/County_Flag_of_Hertfordshire.svg/250px-County_Flag_of_Hertfordshire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:County_Flag_of_Hertfordshire.svg)

The flag of the historic county of Hertfordshire

The first shooting-down of a [zeppelin](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin") over Great Britain during WW1 happened in Cuffley.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

From the [1920s](/wiki/1920s_in_film "1920s in film") until the late [1980s](/wiki/1980s_in_film "1980s in film"), the town of [Borehamwood](/wiki/Borehamwood "Borehamwood") was home to one of the major British [film studio](/wiki/Film_studio "Film studio") complexes, including the [MGM-British Studios](/wiki/MGM-British_Studios "MGM-British Studios"). Many well-known films were made here including the first three *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* movies ([IV](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)"), [V](/wiki/The_Empire_Strikes_Back "The Empire Strikes Back"), & [VI](/wiki/Return_of_the_Jedi "Return of the Jedi")). The studios generally used the name of [Elstree](/wiki/Elstree "Elstree"). American director [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") not only used to shoot in those studios but also lived in the area until his death. *[Big Brother UK](/wiki/Big_Brother_UK "Big Brother UK")* and *[Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?](/wiki/Who_Wants_to_Be_a_Millionaire%3F_(British_game_show) "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire? (British game show)")* have been filmed there. *[EastEnders](/wiki/EastEnders "EastEnders")* is filmed at Elstree. Hertfordshire has seen development at [Warner Bros. Studios, Leavesden](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Studios_Leavesden "Warner Bros. Studios Leavesden"); the [*Harry Potter* series](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)") was filmed here and the 1995 James Bond film *[GoldenEye](/wiki/GoldenEye "GoldenEye")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On 17 October 2000, the [Hatfield rail crash](/wiki/Hatfield_rail_crash "Hatfield rail crash") killed four people with over 70 injured.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The crash exposed the shortcomings of [Railtrack](/wiki/Railtrack "Railtrack"), and resulted in speed restrictions and major track replacement. On 10 May 2002, seven people died in the fourth of the [Potters Bar rail accidents](/wiki/Potters_Bar_rail_accidents "Potters Bar rail accidents"); the train was travelling at high speed when it derailed and flipped into the air when one of the carriages slid along the platform where it came to rest.

In early December 2005, there were [explosions](/wiki/Buncefield_fire "Buncefield fire") at the [Hertfordshire Oil Storage Terminal](/wiki/Hertfordshire_Oil_Storage_Terminal "Hertfordshire Oil Storage Terminal") at Buncefield on the edge of Hemel Hempstead.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Hertfordshire is located in the south-eastern part of England and is the county immediately north of London. It is officially part of the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England") [region, a mainly statistical unit](/wiki/Government_Office_Region "Government Office Region").[[13]](#cite_note-eelga-13) To the east is [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), to the west is [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire") and to the north are [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire") and [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"). A significant minority of the population across all districts [commute](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") to [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London").

The county's boundaries were roughly fixed by the [Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844](/wiki/Counties_(Detached_Parts)_Act_1844#Hertfordshire "Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844") which eliminated [exclaves](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave"); amended when, in 1965 under the [London Government Act 1963](/wiki/London_Government_Act_1963 "London Government Act 1963"), [East Barnet Urban District](/wiki/East_Barnet_Urban_District "East Barnet Urban District") and [Barnet Urban District](/wiki/Barnet_Urban_District "Barnet Urban District") were abolished, their area was transferred to form part of the present-day [London Borough of Barnet](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Barnet "London Borough of Barnet") and the [Potters Bar Urban District](/wiki/Potters_Bar_Urban_District "Potters Bar Urban District") of [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") was transferred to Hertfordshire.

The highest point in the county is at 244 m (801 ft) ([AOD](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level")) on [the Ridgeway](/wiki/The_Ridgeway "The Ridgeway") long distance national path, on the border of [Hastoe](/wiki/Hastoe "Hastoe") near [Tring](/wiki/Tring "Tring") with [Drayton Beauchamp](/wiki/Drayton_Beauchamp "Drayton Beauchamp"), Buckinghamshire.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

At the 2011 census, among the county's ten districts, [East Hertfordshire](/wiki/East_Hertfordshire "East Hertfordshire") had the lowest population density (290 people per km2) while the Borough of [Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford") had the highest (4210 per km2). Compared with neighbouring Bedfordshire and Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire lacks large towns or cities on the scale of [Luton](/wiki/Luton "Luton") or [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes"), whose populations exceed 200,000, but its overall population (1.2 million in 2021) is greater than those of the two aforementioned counties.

The [River Lea](/wiki/River_Lea "River Lea") flows through the county, passing [Harpenden](/wiki/Harpenden "Harpenden"), [Wheathampstead](/wiki/Wheathampstead "Wheathampstead"), Welwyn Garden City, Hertford, Ware, and Broxbourne before reaching [Cheshunt](/wiki/Cheshunt "Cheshunt") and ultimately joining the River Thames. The far west of the county is the most hilly, with the [Chiltern Hills](/wiki/Chiltern_Hills "Chiltern Hills") surrounding [Tring](/wiki/Tring "Tring"), [Berkhamsted](/wiki/Berkhamsted "Berkhamsted") and the Ashridge estate. This [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty") runs from near Hitchin in the north to Berkshire and Oxfordshire.

Many of the county's major settlements are in the central, northern and southern areas, such as Watford, Hemel Hempstead, [Kings Langley](/wiki/Kings_Langley "Kings Langley"), [Rickmansworth](/wiki/Rickmansworth "Rickmansworth"), [St. Albans](/wiki/St._Albans "St. Albans"), [Harpenden](/wiki/Harpenden "Harpenden"), [Redbourn](/wiki/Redbourn "Redbourn"), [Radlett](/wiki/Radlett "Radlett"), [Borehamwood](/wiki/Borehamwood "Borehamwood"), [Potters Bar](/wiki/Potters_Bar "Potters Bar"), Stevenage, [Hatfield](/wiki/Hatfield,_Hertfordshire "Hatfield, Hertfordshire"), [Welwyn](/wiki/Welwyn "Welwyn") and Welwyn Garden City, [Hitchin](/wiki/Hitchin "Hitchin"), Letchworth and Baldock. These are all small to medium-sized locations, featuring a mix of post-WWII new towns and older/more historical locales. The [City of St. Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans") is an example of a historical settlement, as its cathedral and abbey date to the [Norman](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") period, and there are ruins from the Roman settlement of [Verulamium](/wiki/Verulamium "Verulamium") nearby the current city centre. Stevenage is a mix of post-WWII [new town](/wiki/New_town "New town") planning amidst its prior incarnation as a smaller town. The Old Town in Stevenage represents this historic core and has many shops and buildings reflecting its pre-WWII heritage. Hitchin also has a historic centre, with many [Tudor](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor") and [Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") era buildings interspersed amongst more contemporary structures.

Hertfordshire's eastern regions are predominantly rural and arable, intermixed with villages and small to medium-sized towns. Royston, Buntingford and [Bishop's Stortford](/wiki/Bishop%27s_Stortford "Bishop's Stortford"), as well as [Ware](/wiki/Ware,_Hertfordshire "Ware, Hertfordshire") and the county town of Hertford are major settlements in this regard. The area is less elevated than the west, with gently rising and rivers such as the [Stort](/wiki/Stort "Stort"). This river rises in [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") and terminates via a confluence with the Lea near to Ware.
Apart from the Lea and Stort, the River Colne is the major watercourse in the county's west. This runs near Watford and Radlett, and has a complex system/drainage area running south into both Greater London and Buckinghamshire.

An unofficial status, the purple star-shaped flower with yellow stamens, the [Pasqueflower](/wiki/Pulsatilla_vulgaris "Pulsatilla vulgaris") is among endemic [county flowers](/wiki/County_flower "County flower").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main article: [Geology of Hertfordshire](/wiki/Geology_of_Hertfordshire "Geology of Hertfordshire")

The rocks of Hertfordshire belong to the great shallow [syncline](/wiki/Syncline "Syncline") known as the [London Basin](/wiki/London_Basin "London Basin"). The beds dip in a south-easterly direction towards the syncline's lowest point roughly under the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"). The most important formations are the [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") [Chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk"), exposed as the high ground in the north and west of the county, forming the [Chiltern Hills](/wiki/Chiltern_Hills "Chiltern Hills") and the younger [Palaeocene](/wiki/Palaeocene "Palaeocene"), [Reading Beds](/wiki/Reading_Beds "Reading Beds") and [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene"), [London Clay](/wiki/London_Clay "London Clay") which occupy the remaining southern part. The eastern half of the county was covered by glaciers during the [Ice Age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") and has a superficial layer of glacial [boulder clays](/wiki/Boulder_clay "Boulder clay").

### Natural resources and environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Natural resources and environment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/De_Wint%2C_Peter%2C_Cornfields_near_Tring_Station%2C_Hertfordshire%2C_1847.jpg/250px-De_Wint%2C_Peter%2C_Cornfields_near_Tring_Station%2C_Hertfordshire%2C_1847.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_Wint,_Peter,_Cornfields_near_Tring_Station,_Hertfordshire,_1847.jpg)

[Peter de Wint](/wiki/Peter_de_Wint "Peter de Wint"), *Cornfields near Tring Station, Hertfordshire*, 1847, [Princeton University Art Museum](/wiki/Princeton_University_Art_Museum "Princeton University Art Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Hertfordshire_UK_relief_location_map.jpg/250px-Hertfordshire_UK_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hertfordshire_UK_relief_location_map.jpg)

Relief map

Much of the west – and much more in the east – have richly diverse countryside.[[16]](#cite_note-rw-16) These range from beech woods of the [Chilterns](/wiki/Chiltern_Hills "Chiltern Hills"), clayland [buffer zone](/wiki/Buffer_zone "Buffer zone") countryside of Braughing and the Hadhams across to [ancient](/wiki/Ancient_woodland "Ancient woodland") [hornbeam](/wiki/Hornbeam "Hornbeam") [coppices](/wiki/Coppicing "Coppicing") west of the upper Lea valley.[[16]](#cite_note-rw-16) The county has sweeping panoramas of chalklands near [Royston](/wiki/Royston,_Hertfordshire "Royston, Hertfordshire"), [Baldock](/wiki/Baldock "Baldock"), [Hexton](/wiki/Hexton "Hexton") and [Tring](/wiki/Tring "Tring").[[16]](#cite_note-rw-16)

Large parts of the county are used for agriculture.

Some [quarrying](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry") of sand and gravel occurs around St Albans. In the past, clay has supplied local brick-making and still does in [Bovingdon](/wiki/Bovingdon "Bovingdon"), just south-west of Hemel Hempstead. The chalk that is the bedrock of much of the county provides an [aquifer](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer") that feeds streams and is also exploited to provide water supplies for much of the county and beyond. Chalk has also been used as a building material and, once fired, the resultant lime was spread on agricultural land to improve fertility. The mining of chalk since the early 18th century has left unrecorded underground galleries that occasionally collapse unexpectedly and endanger buildings.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Fresh water is supplied to London from [Ware](/wiki/Ware,_Hertfordshire "Ware, Hertfordshire"), using the [New River](/wiki/New_River_(London) "New River (London)") built by [Hugh Myddleton](/wiki/Hugh_Myddleton "Hugh Myddleton") and opened in 1613. Local rivers, although small, supported developing industries such as paper production at [Nash Mills](/wiki/Nash_Mills "Nash Mills").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Hertfordshire affords habitat for a variety of flora and fauna. A bird once common in the shire is the [hooded crow](/wiki/Hooded_Crow#Cultural_significance "Hooded Crow"), the old name of which is the eponymous name of the regional newspaper, the [*Royston Crow*](/wiki/Royston_Crow_(newspaper) "Royston Crow (newspaper)") published in [Royston](/wiki/Royston,_Hertfordshire "Royston, Hertfordshire"). A product, now largely defunct, was [watercress](/wiki/Watercress "Watercress"), based in [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead") and [Berkhamsted](/wiki/Berkhamsted "Berkhamsted") supported by reliable, clean chalk rivers.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Hertfordshire has the main operational and/or headquarters UK site of some very large employers. Clockwise from north:

In [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") (a subsidiary of: [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems"), [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus") and [Finmeccanica](/wiki/Finmeccanica "Finmeccanica")) [MBDA](/wiki/MBDA "MBDA"), develops [missiles](/wiki/Missile "Missile"). In the same town, [Airbus (Defence & Space Division)](/wiki/Airbus_Defence_and_Space "Airbus Defence and Space") produces satellites.

Hatfield was where [de Havilland](/wiki/De_Havilland "De Havilland") developed the first commercial jet liner, the [Comet](/wiki/De_Havilland_Comet "De Havilland Comet"). Now the site is a business park and new campus for the [University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire"). This major employment site notably hosts [EE](/wiki/EE_(telecommunications) "EE (telecommunications)"), [Computacenter](/wiki/Computacenter "Computacenter") and [Ocado](/wiki/Ocado "Ocado") groceries and other goods e-commerce.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Welwyn Garden City](/wiki/Welwyn_Garden_City "Welwyn Garden City") hosts [Tesco](/wiki/Tesco "Tesco")'s UK base, hosts the UK [Cereal Partners](/wiki/Cereal_Partners "Cereal Partners") factory and in pharmaceuticals it hosts Roche UK's headquarters (subsidiary of the Swiss [Hoffman-La Roche](/wiki/Hoffman-La_Roche "Hoffman-La Roche")). [GlaxoSmithKline](/wiki/GlaxoSmithKline "GlaxoSmithKline") has plants in [Ware](/wiki/Ware,_Hertfordshire "Ware, Hertfordshire") and [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead") has large premises of [Dixons Carphone](/wiki/Dixons_Carphone "Dixons Carphone").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The National Pharmacy Association (NPA), the trade association for UK pharmacies, is based in [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Kings Langley](/wiki/Kings_Langley "Kings Langley") has the plant-office of [Pure](/wiki/Pure_Digital "Pure Digital"), making [DAB digital radios](/wiki/Digital_Audio_Broadcasting "Digital Audio Broadcasting").

[Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford") hosts national companies such as [J D Wetherspoon](/wiki/J_D_Wetherspoon "J D Wetherspoon"), [Camelot Group](/wiki/Camelot_Group "Camelot Group"), [Bathstore](/wiki/Bathstore "Bathstore"), and Caversham Finance (BrightHouse). It is also the UK base of multi-nationals [Hilton Worldwide](/wiki/Hilton_Worldwide "Hilton Worldwide"), [TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies"), [TK Maxx](/wiki/TK_Maxx "TK Maxx"), [Costco](/wiki/Costco "Costco"), [JJ Kavanagh and Sons](/wiki/JJ_Kavanagh_and_Sons "JJ Kavanagh and Sons"), [Vinci](/wiki/Vinci_(construction) "Vinci (construction)") and [Beko](/wiki/Beko "Beko"). The 2006 [World Golf Championship](/wiki/World_Golf_Championship "World Golf Championship") and the [2013 Bilderberg Conference](/wiki/2013_Bilderberg_Conference "2013 Bilderberg Conference"), took place at [The Grove hotel](/wiki/The_Grove,_Watford "The Grove, Watford").[[21]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-25) [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") owns and runs its main UK base since the 2000s, Warner Bros. Studios, in Leavesden, Watford.

[Rickmansworth](/wiki/Rickmansworth "Rickmansworth") hosts [Skanska](/wiki/Skanska "Skanska").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sport")]

### Watersports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Watersports")]

[Waltham Cross](/wiki/Waltham_Cross "Waltham Cross"), [Broxbourne](/wiki/Broxbourne_(borough) "Broxbourne (borough)"), is the location of the [Lee Valley White Water Centre](/wiki/Lee_Valley_White_Water_Centre "Lee Valley White Water Centre"), a purpose-built venue opened in 2010 for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). The site consists of two white water courses; one 300m [Grade IV](/wiki/International_scale_of_river_difficulty "International scale of river difficulty") "Olympic" run; and one 160m [Grade III](/wiki/International_scale_of_river_difficulty "International scale of river difficulty") "legacy" run.[[22]](#cite_note-26)

During the games the center was the venue for the [canoe and kayak slalom events](/wiki/Canoeing_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Canoeing at the 2012 Summer Olympics").

Lee Valley has since hosted the [ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships](/wiki/ICF_Canoe_Slalom_World_Championships "ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships") twice. First in [2015](/wiki/2015_ICF_Canoe_Slalom_World_Championships "2015 ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships"), and most recently in [2023](/wiki/2023_ICF_Canoe_Slalom_World_Championships "2023 ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships"), where Britain topped the medal table with 5 golds.

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Vicarage_Road_2015.jpg/250px-Vicarage_Road_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vicarage_Road_2015.jpg)

Vicarage Road stadium in Watford

As of the 2024–25 season, there are four [professional football teams](/wiki/Professionalism_in_association_football "Professionalism in association football") in Hertfordshire: [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."), [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage_F.C. "Stevenage F.C."), [Arsenal WFC](/wiki/Arsenal_W.F.C. "Arsenal W.F.C.") and [Boreham Wood](/wiki/Boreham_Wood_F.C. "Boreham Wood F.C.")

Watford have played their home games at [Vicarage Road](/wiki/Vicarage_Road "Vicarage Road") since 1922.[[23]](#cite_note-27) The club joined the Football League in 1920 as a founding member of the Third Division[[24]](#cite_note-www.watfordfcarchive.com-28) and first played in the First Division of English football in [1982](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Watford_F.C._season "1982–83 Watford F.C. season"), finishing as runners-up to champions [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").[[24]](#cite_note-www.watfordfcarchive.com-28) Watford was promoted to the Premier League at the end of the 2020–2021 season. After spending one season in the Premier League, they were relegated to the Championship again for the 2022–2023 season.[[25]](#cite_note-29)

Stevenage F.C. was formed in 1976 as Stevenage Borough and have played at [Broadhall Way](/wiki/Broadhall_Way "Broadhall Way") since 1980.[[26]](#cite_note-30) Stevenage was the first club to win a competitive match at the new [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), beating [Kidderminster Harriers](/wiki/Kidderminster_Harriers "Kidderminster Harriers") 3–2 in the [2007 FA Trophy Final](/wiki/2007_FA_Trophy_Final "2007 FA Trophy Final").[[27]](#cite_note-31) The club currently play in the [EFL League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") and have been managed by former player [Alex Revell](/wiki/Alex_Revell "Alex Revell") since February 2020.[[28]](#cite_note-32)

[Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), whilst based at the [Emirates Stadium](/wiki/Emirates_Stadium "Emirates Stadium") in the [London Borough of Islington](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Islington "London Borough of Islington"), has long held a training ground in the county. Until 1999, it held the London Colney [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") facility, until it built a new purpose-built compound adjacent to it. Watford FC currently utilises the old Arsenal training area as its training facility.

Arsenal W.F.C. play at [Meadow Park](/wiki/Meadow_Park_(Borehamwood) "Meadow Park (Borehamwood)") in [Borehamwood](/wiki/Borehamwood "Borehamwood").[[29]](#cite_note-33) The club was formed in 1987 and have played in the [FA Women's Super League](/wiki/FA_Women%27s_Super_League "FA Women's Super League") since its inaugural season in [2011](/wiki/2011_FA_WSL "2011 FA WSL").[[30]](#cite_note-34)

Hertfordshire has many semi-professional and amateur clubs. The highest placed are Boreham Wood, [Hemel Hempstead Town](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead_Town_F.C. "Hemel Hempstead Town F.C.") and [St Albans City](/wiki/St_Albans_City_F.C. "St Albans City F.C."), who all play in the [National League South](/wiki/National_League_South "National League South"), the sixth tier of English football.

### Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Rugby")]

#### Rugby league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Rugby league")]

[Hemel Stags](/wiki/Hemel_Stags "Hemel Stags") are a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") team based in [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead").[[31]](#cite_note-35) Hemel Stags have played at [Pennine Way Stadium](/wiki/Pennine_Way_Stadium "Pennine Way Stadium") since the club's founding in 1981.[[32]](#cite_note-36)[[33]](#cite_note-37) Until 2018, the club played in [league 1](/wiki/League_1_(rugby_league) "League 1 (rugby league)"), the [third tier](/wiki/British_rugby_league_system#Tier_3:_League_1 "British rugby league system") of the British rugby league system, and now compete in the [Conference League South](/wiki/Conference_League_South "Conference League South").[[34]](#cite_note-38)

#### Rugby union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Rugby union")]

The [Hertfordshire Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Hertfordshire_Rugby_Football_Union "Hertfordshire Rugby Football Union") is the governing body for [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") in Hertfordshire and is responsible for any interested parties involved in rugby.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

[Tring Rugby](/wiki/Tring_Rugby "Tring Rugby") play matches at Cow Lane, Tring.[[36]](#cite_note-40) The first XV currently play in the [Regional 1 South East](/wiki/Regional_1_South_East "Regional 1 South East"),[[37]](#cite_note-41) League. A [level 5](/wiki/English_rugby_union_system#Level_5:_Regional_1_South_East "English rugby union system") league.

#### GAA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: GAA")]

[Gaelic Football](/wiki/Gaelic_Football "Gaelic Football") is played within Hertfordshire, with clubs from [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire") all the way to [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") playing in the Hertfordshire League and Championship. Eire Óg, Oxford are the 2022 County Champions. [Hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") is played by an amalgamated team, St Declan's CLG, with players contributed from all football teams across Hertfordshire. St Declan's currently play in the [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") League and Championship, having previously played in the [London GAA](/wiki/London_GAA "London GAA") championship.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Netball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Netball")]

[London Mavericks](/wiki/London_Mavericks "London Mavericks"), previously Hertfordshire Mavericks and Saracens Mavericks, have competed in the [Netball Super League](/wiki/Netball_Super_League_(United_Kingdom) "Netball Super League (United Kingdom)") since 2005.[[38]](#cite_note-:0-42) The franchise represents the East region and plays a number of home fixtures at the [University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire") sports village.[[39]](#cite_note-43)[[40]](#cite_note-44) Mavericks have appeared in the Netball Super League Grand Final seven times, winning the title in both 2008 and 2011.[[38]](#cite_note-:0-42)

Turnford Netball Club and Hatfield Netball Club are both netball teams from Hertfordshire which play in the [England Netball Premier League](/wiki/England_Netball_Premier_League "England Netball Premier League"), the highest level of club/amateur netball in the country.[[41]](#cite_note-45)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/M25-M1_intersection_near_Hemel_Hempstead.jpg/250px-M25-M1_intersection_near_Hemel_Hempstead.jpg)](/wiki/File:M25-M1_intersection_near_Hemel_Hempstead.jpg)

Junction of the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") and [M25](/wiki/M25_motorway "M25 motorway") near [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/700110_-_London_Blackfriars_3T13.JPG/250px-700110_-_London_Blackfriars_3T13.JPG)](/wiki/File:700110_-_London_Blackfriars_3T13.JPG)

[Govia Thameslink Railway](/wiki/Govia_Thameslink_Railway "Govia Thameslink Railway") provide frequent train services through Hertfordshire on the [Midland Main Line](/wiki/Midland_Main_Line "Midland Main Line") and [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Bridge_168%2C_Grand_Union_Canal%2C_Watford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_464942.jpg/250px-Bridge_168%2C_Grand_Union_Canal%2C_Watford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_464942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bridge_168,_Grand_Union_Canal,_Watford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_464942.jpg)

Bridge 168 on the [Grand Union Canal](/wiki/Grand_Union_Canal "Grand Union Canal")

Hertfordshire is a [home county](/wiki/Home_county "Home county") with many towns forming part of the [London commuter belt](/wiki/London_commuter_belt "London commuter belt") and has some of the principal roads in England including the [A1](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)"), [A1(M)](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)"), [A41](/wiki/A41_road "A41 road"), [A414](/wiki/A414_road "A414 road"), [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway"), [M11](/wiki/M11_motorway "M11 motorway"), and the [M25](/wiki/M25_motorway "M25 motorway").

Four principal national railway lines pass through the county:

* the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line") from [Euston](/wiki/Euston_railway_station "Euston railway station"). [Avanti West Coast](/wiki/Avanti_West_Coast "Avanti West Coast") operates high speed [intercity](/wiki/InterCity_(British_Rail) "InterCity (British Rail)") services via [Watford Junction](/wiki/Watford_Junction_railway_station "Watford Junction railway station") to [the Midlands](/wiki/The_Midlands "The Midlands"), [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales"), the [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). [West Midlands Trains](/wiki/West_Midlands_Trains "West Midlands Trains") provides local commuter and regional services.
* the [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line") from [London King's Cross](/wiki/London_King%27s_Cross_railway_station "London King's Cross railway station"). Local commuter and regional services are provided by [Govia Thameslink Railway](/wiki/Govia_Thameslink_Railway "Govia Thameslink Railway"). [London North Eastern Railway](/wiki/London_North_Eastern_Railway "London North Eastern Railway") runs high speed intercity services via [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage_railway_station "Stevenage railway station") to the east coast of [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")
* the [Midland Main Line](/wiki/Midland_Main_Line "Midland Main Line") which forms part of the [Thameslink route](/wiki/Thameslink "Thameslink") between [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford_railway_station "Bedford railway station") and [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton_railway_station "Brighton railway station") via [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London") with services are provided by [Govia Thameslink Railway](/wiki/Govia_Thameslink_Railway "Govia Thameslink Railway"). [East Midlands Railway](/wiki/East_Midlands_Railway "East Midlands Railway") provide intercity services along the line from [London St Pancras](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station") to the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and Yorkshire
* the [West Anglia Main Line](/wiki/West_Anglia_Main_Line "West Anglia Main Line") from [London Liverpool Street](/wiki/Liverpool_Street_station "Liverpool Street station"). Local commuter and regional services are provided by [Greater Anglia](/wiki/Greater_Anglia_(train_operating_company) "Greater Anglia (train operating company)") mainly in the east of the county

A number of other local rail routes also cross Hertfordshire:

* the [London to Aylesbury Line](/wiki/London_to_Aylesbury_Line "London to Aylesbury Line") from [London Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone_railway_station "Marylebone railway station") runs via [Rickmansworth](/wiki/Rickmansworth_station "Rickmansworth station") and [Chorleywood](/wiki/Chorleywood_station "Chorleywood station")
* the [Abbey Line](/wiki/Abbey_Line "Abbey Line"), a local line from Watford to [St Albans Abbey](/wiki/St_Albans_Abbey_railway_station "St Albans Abbey railway station")
* the [Cambridge Line](/wiki/Cambridge_Line "Cambridge Line"), a branch of the East Coast line which runs via [Royston](/wiki/Royston_railway_station "Royston railway station") and [Letchworth](/wiki/Letchworth_Garden_City_railway_station "Letchworth Garden City railway station") to [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_railway_station "Cambridge railway station")

Three commuter lines operated by [Transport for London](/wiki/Transport_for_London "Transport for London") enter the county:

* the [Lea Valley Lines](/wiki/Lea_Valley_Lines "Lea Valley Lines"), a suburban metro line from Liverpool Street to [Cheshunt](/wiki/Cheshunt_railway_station "Cheshunt railway station") via [Seven Sisters](/wiki/Seven_Sisters_railway_station "Seven Sisters railway station")
* the [Watford DC Line](/wiki/Watford_DC_Line "Watford DC Line"), a suburban metro line from Euston to Watford Junction
* five stations on the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground") [Metropolitan line](/wiki/Metropolitan_line "Metropolitan line")

The distance travelled by buses in Hertfordshire has reduced by 56.5% since 2017.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

[Stansted Airport](/wiki/Stansted_Airport "Stansted Airport") and [Luton Airport](/wiki/Luton_Airport "Luton Airport") are both within 10 miles (16 km) of the county's borders in Essex and Bedfordshire, respectively. The commercial airfield at [Elstree](/wiki/Elstree "Elstree") is for light aircraft.

The [Grand Union Canal](/wiki/Grand_Union_Canal "Grand Union Canal") passes through [Rickmansworth](/wiki/Rickmansworth "Rickmansworth"), [Watford](/wiki/Watford "Watford"), [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead"), [Berkhamsted](/wiki/Berkhamsted "Berkhamsted") and [Tring](/wiki/Tring "Tring").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/University_of_Hertfordshire_building_1.jpg/250px-University_of_Hertfordshire_building_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Hertfordshire_building_1.jpg)

[University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire")

See also: [List of schools in Hertfordshire](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Hertfordshire "List of schools in Hertfordshire")

Hertfordshire has 26 independent schools and 73 state secondary schools.
The state secondary schools are entirely [comprehensive](/wiki/Comprehensive_school "Comprehensive school"), although 7 schools in the south and southwest of the county are [partially selective](/wiki/Partially_selective_school_(England) "Partially selective school (England)") (see [Education in Watford](/wiki/Watford#Education "Watford")).
All state schools have [sixth forms](/wiki/Sixth_form "Sixth form"), and there are no [sixth form colleges](/wiki/Sixth_form_college "Sixth form college").
The tertiary colleges, each with multiple campuses, are [Hertford Regional College](/wiki/Hertford_Regional_College "Hertford Regional College"), [North Hertfordshire College](/wiki/North_Hertfordshire_College "North Hertfordshire College"), [Oaklands College](/wiki/Oaklands_College "Oaklands College") and [West Herts College](/wiki/West_Herts_College "West Herts College").
The [University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire") is a modern university based largely in [Hatfield](/wiki/Hatfield,_Hertfordshire "Hatfield, Hertfordshire"). It has more than 23,000 students.

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertfordshire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Literature")]

Hertfordshire is the location of [Jack Worthing](/wiki/Jack_Worthing "Jack Worthing")'s country house in [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde")'s play *[The Importance of Being Earnest](/wiki/The_Importance_of_Being_Earnest "The Importance of Being Earnest")*.

[Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen")'s novel *[Pride and Prejudice](/wiki/Pride_and_Prejudice_(novel) "Pride and Prejudice (novel)")* is primarily set in Hertfordshire.[[43]](#cite_note-47)

The location of Mr Jarndyce's Bleak House in [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens")'s *[Bleak House](/wiki/Bleak_House "Bleak House")* is near St Albans.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

The eponymous residence in [E. M. Forster](/wiki/E._M._Forster "E. M. Forster")'s novel *[Howards End](/wiki/Howards_End "Howards End")* was based on [Rooks Nest House](/wiki/Rooks_Nest_House "Rooks Nest House") just outside [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage").[[45]](#cite_note-49)

[George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") based *[Animal Farm](/wiki/Animal_Farm "Animal Farm")* on [Wallington, Hertfordshire](/wiki/Wallington,_Hertfordshire "Wallington, Hertfordshire"), where he lived between 1936 and 1940. Manor Farm and The Great Barn both feature in the novel.[[46]](#cite_note-50)[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-52)
